data.body:
[G4 child-consistency — container review of parent 1f19-adf2-ffb2-4b09 paired with THIS child (S1 6d56: Faithful, lock-free snapshot materialization core); against the WHOLE EIGHT-child roster (S1 6d56 THIS, S2 70c7 cache, S2b b73c janitor, S3 fa49 gate threading, S4 30b3 attested signing, S4b e308 claim-gate ref-coherence, S5 b7fa CLI/MCP surfaces, S6 1ba5 docs)]

ONE high-confidence finding (conflicting assumptions, mode 2 — the S1<->S3 local-mode seam). The other 6 modes are clean under the high-confidence / benign-reading filter.

FINDING (conflicting assumptions — does materialize() run in local mode? S1's API says yes; S3 says local never materializes)
- finding: THIS child exposes `materialize(ref, *, source_mode) -> handle` — `source_mode` (attested|local) is a PARAMETER of the materialize core, so the core dispatches on it — and its Descriptive-errors What-bullet specifies per-mode behavior INSIDE materialize: "a fetch that fails on auth surfaces an actionable credential message ...; attested mode fails closed, LOCAL MODE STILL RUNS" (echoed by AC5 "...attested mode fails closed"). This places a local-mode branch (and a tolerated/fail-soft fetch) inside materialize(), i.e. materialize() is entered in BOTH modes. S3 (fa49) states the OPPOSITE and owns the gate-side mode dispatch: What bullet 3 "Local: read the server's in-place checkout directly (NO MATERIALIZATION, NO CACHE); mark the run as unsigned"; AC4 "In local mode repo_root IS the read root (the checkout); in attested mode repo_root only locates the object DB and the snapshot is the read root"; Scope In "local-mode in-place read." Under S3, local mode NEVER enters materialize() and never fetches. S2 (70c7) concurs that local bypasses the cache. So "does materialize() run in local mode?" is answered oppositely by S1 (yes — it has a source_mode param + a local fetch-error branch) and S3 (no — local is an in-place read that never materializes).
- criteria: this child (6d56) `materialize(ref, *, source_mode)` signature + "Descriptive errors" What-bullet ("attested mode fails closed, local mode still runs") + AC5; S3 (fa49) What bullet 3 ("Local: ... no materialization, no cache") + AC4 ("local mode ... repo_root IS the read root") + Scope In ("local-mode in-place read"); parent AC3 ("local source mode reads the in-place checkout and CANNOT produce a signed verdict").
- location: 6d56 "What" (the `materialize(ref, *, source_mode) -> handle` signature + the Descriptive-errors bullet) + AC5; fa49 "What" (Attested vs Local bullets) + AC4 + Scope ("In: ... local-mode in-place read").
- evidence:
  1. 6d56 signature is `materialize(ref, *, source_mode) -> handle` — source_mode is a parameter of the materialize core, implying the core branches on it.
  2. 6d56 Descriptive-errors bullet: "attested mode fails closed, local mode still runs" — a local-mode behavior described as materialize()'s own responsibility (a per-mode branch on the fetch path that materialize() owns).
  3. fa49 What bullet 3: "Local: read the server's in-place checkout directly (no materialization, no cache); mark the run as unsigned" — local mode explicitly does NOT enter materialization.
  4. fa49 AC4: "In local mode repo_root IS the read root (the checkout)"; fa49 Scope In: "local-mode in-place read" — the in-place read is CLAIMED by S3, not S1.
  5. Neither ticket reconciles the two: neither states whether materialize() is a local-mode short-circuit returning a handle over the in-place checkout, or whether S3 branches BEFORE ever calling materialize(). The "fail-soft fetch" benign reading does not rescue it: if local never enters materialize() (S3's model), there is no fetch on the local path for S1's "local mode still runs" branch to apply to, so that branch + the local arm of `source_mode` have no caller.
- scenarios: (a) The S1 implementer builds source_mode dispatch + a local branch into materialize() that returns an in-place-checkout handle; the S3 implementer ALSO builds a local in-place read in the gate dispatch (its claimed Scope/AC4) -> the in-place read is DOUBLE-IMPLEMENTED, and the two may diverge on the read root (snapshot tmp seam vs cfg.repo_path/repo_root), and S2's cache/single-flight could wrap a local dirty read it was never meant to touch. (b) The S3 implementer short-circuits local BEFORE materialize() per its "no materialization" wording; then S1's `source_mode` parameter and its "local mode still runs" error-branch are DEAD code no caller exercises, and S1 AC5's local clause cannot be tested through the real gate path.
- impact: Ambiguous ownership of the local-mode in-place-read path across the S1<->S3 seam. Parent AC3 ("local reads the in-place checkout") is claimed by S3, but THIS child's API surface (source_mode param) + AC5/What also reach into local mode, so the boundary "does materialize() run in local mode?" is answered oppositely by the two tickets — yielding either dead surface in S1 or double-implementation + possible read-root divergence in S3. The local-mode behavior is under-determined.
- checklist_item: conflicting assumptions (cross-child mode 2) at the S1<->S3 local-mode seam
- suggested_fix: Pick ONE boundary and state it in both tickets. Preferred (matches S3's explicit wording): S3 branches on source BEFORE calling S1 — local never enters materialize(); then DROP `source_mode` from S1's signature (make it attested-only, `materialize(ref) -> handle`) and reword S1's Descriptive-errors bullet/AC5 to "attested mode fails closed" only, deleting the "local mode still runs" clause (that behavior belongs to S3 AC4). Alternative: if materialize() is to own the local short-circuit, change S3 to "Local: call materialize(..., source_mode=local), which returns a handle over the in-place checkout (no fetch, no cache)" and drop S3's independent in-place-read claim, so the in-place read is implemented exactly once. Either way, exactly one child owns the local-vs-attested branch and the other's wording must match.

Clean on the other 6 modes:
- implicit shared state — CLEAN. THIS child is the PRODUCER of the snapshot dir + resolved immutable SHA + the materialize() handle + the atomic dir-rename primitive + the tmp/ residue; consumed coherently and mutually named: S2 caches the entry ("populated via S1's atomic dir-rename"; "Out: materialization itself (S1)") and owns the cache root layout + startup sweep ("rmtree tmp/* trash/*"); S2b janitor reclaims it; S3 re-roots gate file tools onto the snapshot dir; S4 signs over the resolved SHA; S5 surfaces it; S6 documents the credential error + portability knobs. The flock+atomic-mkdir-fallback portability seam is ESTABLISHED here and RIDDEN by S2's cross-process flock(LOCK_EX)/S2b's gc/lock without re-ownership. No shared-state value is produced-without-consumer or consumed-without-producer (the local-read overlap is captured as the mode-2 finding above, not re-counted here).
- dependency gap — CLEAN at this child. materialize()->handle is consumed by S2 (cache populate) and S3 (gate re-root), both naming S1; the resolved SHA by S4/S5; portability knobs by S6. The fetch-prerequisite note (uploadpack.allowReachableSHA1InWant) is self-contained — S1 supplies a fallback ("or fetch a containing ref then resolve"). The standing roster dependency-gap (S4/S3 routing the pinned-SHA snapshot handle into plan_review/attest.py's dependency_hashes()/_rehash()/freshness re-check, all rooted on _config.repo_root per attest.py:106-107/:191-196/:325-326) is DOWNSTREAM of this child's resolved-SHA output, on the consuming (S3/S4/S4b) side — S1 correctly PRODUCES the handle; not originated here.
- scope overlap — CLEAN. S1's "Coalesced git fetch origin" (coalescing the lock-taking fetch) is a DISTINCT layer from S2's "single-flight per SHA" (coalescing materialization for a given SHA); S1 Scope "Out: the cache/janitor (S2)" vs S2 Scope "Out: materialization itself (S1)" — clean producer/cache split. Parent AC4 splits cleanly: S1 owns atomic-population + no-worktree + no index.lock/config.lock contention (AC3/AC6); S2 owns single-flight + delete-on-last-close; S2b owns janitor reclamation. The flock portability seam is established here and ridden, not re-owned. No double-implementation.
- ordering violation — CLEAN. This child's deps encode `blocks 70c7` (S2) and `blocks fa49` (S3) — i.e. S1 -> S2/S3, coherent with the producer->consumer flow (materialize core precedes the cache that populates from it and the gates that re-root onto it). Parent AC9 only requires all-closed + green; no encoded ordering is violated.
- consumer impact — CLEAN. S1's materialize(ref) -> handle contract + resolved-immutable-SHA are consumed by S2 (cache key), S3 (re-root; its AC6 tolerates ENOENT/being-GC'd via re-materialize, consistent with S2's delete-on-last-close model), S4 (sign over verified_at_sha), S5 (surface), S6 (document). LFS-pointer detection + defined submodule handling (AC2) feed S4's "this is the code at SHA X" attestation faithfully; no downstream contract is broken by this child's surface.
- residual references — CLEAN. "see S6" (credential message -> S6 AC2), S2/S3/S4/S5 scope-outs (all present in roster), REBAR_GATE_TMPDIR (documented by S6 AC5), uploadpack.allowReachableSHA1InWant / --filter=blob:none (real git config), the EFS/NFS flock caveat (tracked on alto-fruit-punch per the parent Out-of-scope) all resolve.

Note: the materialization module (src/rebar/_engine/repo_snapshot.py) does not yet exist in the tree (pre-manifest epic), so the finding is grounded in the ticket-spec wording of the S1<->S3 seam, not in shipped code.

Conclusion: one high-confidence cross-child inconsistency stands (conflicting assumptions, mode 2 — THIS child's materialize() advertises a `source_mode` parameter and a "local mode still runs" fetch-error branch, while S3 routes local mode entirely around materialization, "no materialization, no cache; repo_root IS the read root"; the two disagree on whether materialize() participates in local mode). The remaining 6 modes are clean.
